chapter.p: Vance had not seen Lewis Tarrant for a long time. Tarrant was at the office less often than formerly; the practical administration of the New Hour was entrusted to Eric Rauch, who ran it according to his own ideas save when his chief, unexpectedly turning up, upset existing arrangements and substituted new plans of his own. This state of things did not escape the notice of the small staff. It was rumoured that Tarrant was losing interestвБ†вАФthen again that he was absorbed in the writing of a novel; it was agreed that, in any case, something was wrong with his nerves. Handling Tarrant had always been a gingerly business; Rauch was the only man supple enough to restore his good-humour without yielding to him; and even Rauch (as he told Vance) never knew beforehand whether he was going to pull it off or not. вАЬThe trouble is, heвАЩs got enough ideas to run a dozen reviews; and he wants to apply them all to this one poor rag.вАЭ The result was that the rag was drooping; and as soon as an enterprise gave the least sign of failure it was TarrantвАЩs instinct to disclaim all responsibility for it.

His frequent absences had been a relief to Vance. When the two did meet, though usually it was only to exchange a few words, Vance felt unhappy in the otherвАЩs presence. To anyone less simplehearted it might have been easier to despise the husband of the woman he worshipped than to be on friendly terms with him; but his opinion of Tarrant added to VanceвАЩs general distress of mind. It made him utterly wretched to think of Halo TarrantвАЩs life being spent with a man for whom she must feel the same contempt that Tarrant excited in him; and since his last talk with herвБ†вАФsince she had uttered that вАЬI wonвАЩt take a lover while I have a husbandвАЭ which still filled him with its poignant musicвБ†вАФhis feeling for her husband had hardened to hatred. His own grievance against Tarrant played little part in this, except insofar as it showed him the kind of man she was chained to; but he wondered bitterly how she could consider the tie binding. In his own case it was different; he was bound to Laura Lou by her helplessness and his own folly, and the bond seemed to him so sacred that he had at once acknowledged the force of HaloвАЩs argument. He knew he could not abandon his wife for the woman he loved; he owned the impossibility and bowed to it. But HaloвАЩs case was different. Tarrant was not an object of pity; and what other feeling could hold her to him?

It was a strange and desperate coil, but one on which his immediate domestic problem left him no time to brood. He was still determined to do all he could to prevent his grandmother from continuing her lecturing tour; even if he could not prevail with her, and she did continue it, he was determined that his wife and he should not live on her earnings. Whatever the alternative might beвБ†вАФand all seemed hopelessвБ†вАФthat resolve was fixed in him.

As soon as his grandmother had gone he decided to look up the publisher who had made him such urgent proposals. He carried the hundred dollars up to Laura Lou, and let his heart be warmed for an instant by her cry of surprise, and the assurance that sheвАЩd never seen anybody as lovely as his grandmother; then he hurried off on his errand. But at the door he ran into a messenger from the New Hour, with a line from Rauch saying that Tarrant wished to see him about something important. вАЬBetter come along as quick as you can,вАЭ a postscript added; and Vance unwillingly turned toward the office.

He had not realized till that moment how deeply distasteful it would be to him to see Tarrant again. He had no time to wonder what the object of the summons might be; the mere physical reluctance to stand in the manвАЩs presence overcame all conjecture, filled him to the throat with disgust at being at his orders. вАЬThatвАЩs got to end too,вАЭ he thoughtвБ†вАФand then it occurred to him that the Mr. LambartвАЩs negotiations might have been successful, and that Tarrant, as conscious as himself of the friction between them, had decided to give him his release.

This carried him to the office on lighter feet, and he entered the editorial retreat with a feeling almost of reassurance. It would be hateful, being near Tarrant, seeing him, hearing himвБ†вАФbut the ordeal would doubtless soon be over.

Tarrant looked up quickly from his desk: it was one of the days when his face was like a perfectly symmetrical but shuttered housefront. вАЬSit down,вАЭ he sighed; and then: вАЬLook here, Weston, IвАЩm afraid youвАЩll have to move to some place where I can reach you by telephone. ItвАЩs the devil and all trying to get at you; and your hours at the office are so irregularвБ†вАФвАЭ

VanceвАЩs heart sank. That dry even voice was even more detestable to listen to than he had expected. He hardly knew which part of TarrantвАЩs challenge to answer first; but finally he said, ineffectively: вАЬI can work better away from the office.вАЭ This was not true, for he did most of his writing there; but he was too bewildered to remember it.
Tarrant smiled drily. вАЬWell, that remains to be seen. ItвАЩs so long since any of your script has been visible here that IвАЩve no means of knowing how much of the new book youвАЩve turned out.вАЭ
VanceвАЩs blood was up. вАЬI donвАЩt understand. It was with your consent that I dropped the monthly articles to give all my time to my novelвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬOh, just so,вАЭ Tarrant interrupted suavely. вАЬAnd I presume youвАЩve got along with it fairly well, as I understand youвАЩve been negotiating privately to sell it to Lambart.вАЭ

вАЬIt was Lambart who came to me. He offered to fix it up with Dreck and Saltzer. The price I had contracted for with them is so much below what I understand I have a right to expect that I couldnвАЩt afford to refuse.вАЭ

вАЬRefuse what? Are you under the impression that you can sell the same book twice over, to two different publishers? You signed a contract some time ago with Dreck and Saltzer for all your literary output for four yearsвБ†вАФand that contract has over two years more to run. Dreck and Saltzer have asked me for an explanation because it was through me that the New Hour was able to put you in the way of securing a publisher. TheyвАЩre not used to this way of doing business; neither am I, I confess.вАЭ

Vance was silent. The blood was beating angrily in his temples; but, put thus baldly, he could not but feel that his action seemed underhand, if not actually dishonourable. Of course he ought not to have concealed from Tarrant and his publishers that he had authorized Lambart to negotiate for him. He saw clearly that what he had done was open to misconstruction; but his personal antagonism toward Tarrant robbed him of his self-control. вАЬThe price I was to get from Dreck and Saltzer wasnвАЩt a living wageвБ†вАФвАЭ

Tarrant leaned back in his chair, and drummed on the desk with long impatient fingers. His hands were bloodless but delicately muscular: he wore a dark red seal ring on his left fourth finger. With those fingers he had pushed back his wifeвАЩs hair from the temples, where Vance had so often watched the pulses beatвБ† вБ†вА¶ had traced the little blue veins that netted them.вБ† вБ†вА¶ VanceвАЩs eyes were blurred with rage.

вАЬWhy did you sign the contract if you werenвАЩt satisfied with it?вАЭ Tarrant continued, in his carefully restrained voice. It was evident that he was very angry, but that the crude expression of his wrath would have given him no more pleasure than an unskilful stroke gives a good tennis player. His very quietness increased VanceвАЩs sense of inferiority.
вАЬI signed because I was a beginner, because I had to.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ

Tarrant paused and stretched his hand toward a cigarette. вАЬWell, youвАЩre a beginner stillвБ† вБ†вА¶ youвАЩve got to remember that.вБ† вБ†вА¶ You missed the Pulsifer Prize for your short story: for that blunder youвАЩll agree weвАЩre not responsible; but it did us a lot more harm than it did you. We were counting on the prize to give you a boost, to make you a more valuable asset, as it were. And there was every reason to think you would have got it if you hadnвАЩt tried to extract the money out of Mrs. Pulsifer in advance.вАЭ Vance crimsoned, and stammered out: вАЬOh, see hereвБ†вАФвАЭ; but Tarrant ignored the interruption. вАЬAfter that you dropped doing the monthly articles youвАЩd contracted to supply us with, in order to have more time for this new novel. In short, as far as the New Hour is concerned, ever since the serial publication of Instead came to an end weвАЩve been, so to speak, keeping you as a luxury.вАЭ He paused, lit the cigarette, and proffered the box to Vance, who waved it impatiently aside. вАЬOh, you understand; we accepted the situation willingly. It has always been our policy to make allowances for the artistic temperamentвБ†вАФto give our contributors a free hand. But we rather expect a square deal in return. If any of our authors are dissatisfied we prefer to hear of it from themselves; and so do Dreck and Saltzer. We donвАЩt care to find out from outsiders that the books promised to usвБ†вАФand of which the serial rights are partly paid in advanceвБ†вАФare being hawked about in other publishersвАЩ offices without our knowledge. That sort of thing does no good to an authorвБ†вАФand a lot of harm to us.вАЭ He ended his statement with a slight cough, and paused for VanceвАЩs answer.

Vance was trembling with anger and mortification. He knew that Tarrant had made out a case for himself, and yet that whatever wrong he, Vance, had done in the matter, came out of the initial wrong perpetrated against him by his editor and publisher. But he could not find words in which to put all this consecutively and convincingly. The allusion to his attempt to borrow money from Mrs. Pulsifer, the discovery of her having betrayed the fact, were so sickening that he hardly noticed TarrantвАЩs cynical avowal that, but for this, they could have captured the prize for him. His head was whirling with confused arguments, but he had sense enough left to reflect: вАЬI mustnвАЩt let go of myself.вБ† вБ†вА¶ I must try and look as cool as he does.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ

Finally he said: вАЬYou say you and Dreck and Saltzer want to be fair to your authors. Well, the contract you advised me to make with them wasnвАЩt a fair one. Instead was a success, and theyвАЩve wriggled out of paying the royalties theyвАЩd agreed on on the ground that theyвАЩve lost money on the book because itвАЩs not a full-length novel.вАЭ
вАЬBut didnвАЩt your contract with them specify that the scale of royalties that you accepted applied only to a full-length novel?вАЭ
вАЬWell, I suppose it did. I guess I didnвАЩt read it very carefully. But they must have made a lot more money on Instead than they expected.вАЭ

Tarrant leaned back in his chair and laid his fingertips together, with the gently argumentative air of one who reasons with the unreasonable. вАЬMy dear Weston,вАЭ he began; and Vance winced at the apostrophe. All authors, Tarrant went onвБ†вАФyoung authors, that isвБ†вАФthought there was nothing easier than to decide exactly how much money their editors and publishers were making out of them. And they always worked out the account to their own disadvantageвБ†вАФnaturally. In reality it wasnвАЩt as simple as that, and editors and publishers often stood to lose the very sums the authors accused them of raking in. A book might have a lot of fuss made about it in a small circleвБ†вАФInstead was just such a caseвБ†вАФand yet you couldnвАЩt get the big public to buy it. And unluckily it was only the favour of the big public that made a book pay. As a matter of fact, Instead was a loss to Dreck and Saltzer. If Weston would write another book just like it, but of the proper length, very likely theyвАЩd make up their deficit on that; a highbrow success on a first book often helped the sales of the next, provided it was the right length. Dreck and Saltzer knew this, and though they were actually out of pocket they were ready to have another try.вБ† вБ†вА¶ Publishing was just one long gamble.вБ† вБ†вА¶ And perhaps it wasnвАЩt unfair to remind Weston that, both to the New Hour and to Dreck and Saltzer, heвАЩd so far been, from the business point of view, rather a heavy load to carry.вБ† вБ†вА¶

Vance had controlled himself by a violent effort of the will; but at this summing-up of the case he broke out. вАЬWell, I daresay I haveвБ†вАФbut why not let me off our contract, if itвАЩs been as much of a disappointment to you as to me?вАЭ
TarrantвАЩs slow blood rose to his cheeks. вАЬIвАЩm curious to know why you consider yourself disappointed.вАЭ

вАЬWhy, for the reasons IвАЩve told you. My articles havenвАЩt been a successвБ†вАФI know that as well as you. But my book has, and under our agreement itвАЩs brought me no more than if it had been a failure. I canвАЩt live, and keep my wife alive, on the salary you give me, and if youвАЩll let me off I can earn three times the money tomorrow.вАЭ

Tarrant was silent. He began to drum again on the desk, and the dull red of anger still coloured his pale skin. вАЬMy dear fellow, youвАЩve had plenty of opportunities to complain to me of our contract, and it never seems to have occurred to you to do so till that pirate Lambart came along. Even then, if youвАЩd come straight to me and stated your case frankly, I donвАЩt sayвБ† вБ†вА¶ But IвАЩm not in the habit of letting my contributors be bribed away behind my back, and neither are Dreck and Saltzer. You signed a contract with us, and that contract holds.вАЭ
вАЬWhy do you want it to hold?вАЭ
Tarrant continued his nervous drumming. вАЬThatвАЩs our own business.вАЭ

вАЬWell, if you wonвАЩt answer, IвАЩll answer for you. ItвАЩs because IвАЩve given you one success at a bargain, and you feel you may miss another if you set me free. And it pays you to hang on to me on that chance.вАЭ

TarrantвАЩs lips moved slowly before his answer became audible. вАЬYesвБ†вАФI suppose thatвАЩs the view certain people might take.вБ† вБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs one that doesnвАЩt enter into our way of doing business. Besides, before knowing whether the chance you speak of was worth gambling on, as you assert, I should have had to see the book youвАЩre at work on now; it doesnвАЩt always happen that beginners follow up a first success with a second. Rather the other way round.вАЭ
вАЬThen why wonвАЩt you let me go?вАЭ
Tarrant, instead of replying, lit another cigarette, puffed at it for a moment, and then said: вАЬBy the way, is your manuscript here?вАЭ
вАЬYes.вАЭ
вАЬI should like to have a look at itвБ† вБ†вА¶ only for an hour or two. So far I havenвАЩt much idea of what itвАЩs about.вАЭ
Vance pulled the manuscript out of his pocket. вАЬIf you donвАЩt like it, will you let me off?вАЭ he repeated doggedly.
вАЬNo, certainly not. I shall ask you to make an effort to give us something more satisfactory, as I have every right to.вАЭ
There was another pause. The air between the two men seemed to Vance to become suddenly rarefied, as if nothing intervened to deflect the swift currents of their antagonism.
вАЬYou wonвАЩt like it,вАЭ Vance insisted with white lips.
вАЬI daresay not. No doubt youвАЩve seen to that.вАЭ
The sneer struck Vance like a blow. He felt powerless with wrath and humiliation.

вАЬItвАЩs no use your reading it anyhow,вАЭ he exclaimed, no longer knowing what he was saying. He leaned across the desk, snatched up the pages, and tore them to bits before TarrantвАЩs astonished eyes. He could not stop tearingвБ†вАФit seemed as if the bits would never be small enough to ensure the complete annihilation of his work.

He was conscious that Tarrant, after the first shock of surprise, was watching him with a sort of cold disgust; and also that, when the work of destruction was over, their relative situations would be exactly what they had been before. But this cool appreciation of the case was far below the surface of his emotions. He could not resist the sombre physical satisfaction of destroying under that manвАЩs eyes what he had made.вБ† вБ†вА¶
The last scrap dropped to the floor, and Tarrant said quietly: вАЬIвАЩm afraid now youвАЩll have to send round and get the copy you have at home.вАЭ
вАЬIвАЩve no other copy,вАЭ Vance retorted.
вАЬThatвАЩs a pity. YouвАЩve given yourself a lot of unnecessary workвБ†вАФand IвАЩm damned if I see why. WhatвАЩs the sense of having to begin the thing all over again?вАЭ
вАЬI shall never begin it over again.вАЭ

вАЬWell, if you werenвАЩt satisfied with it, or thought it wouldnвАЩt suit our purpose, I daresay youвАЩre right. But in that case IвАЩll have to ask you to buckle down and turn out something else in the shortest possible time. WeвАЩve been a good many months now without getting any return for our money.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ
вАЬI shall never write anything for you again,вАЭ said Vance slowly.

Tarrant did not speak for a moment or two. His colour had faded to its usual ivory-like sallowness, and the furrow deepened between his ironically lifted eyebrows. He had the immense advantage over his antagonist that anger made him cold instead of hot.
вАЬNever? YouвАЩd better think that over, hadnвАЩt you? You understand, of course, that your not writing for us wonвАЩt set you free to write for anybody else till the four years are over.вАЭ
Vance looked at him with something of the otherвАЩs own chill contempt. His wrath had dropped; he felt only immeasurably repelled.
вАЬYou mean, then,вАЭ he said, вАЬthat even if I donвАЩt write another line for you youвАЩll hold onto me?вАЭ
вАЬIвАЩm afraid youвАЩve left me no alternative,вАЭ Tarrant answered coldly. He rang the bell on his desk, and said to the office boy who appeared: вАЬClear up those papers, will you?вАЭ

In the street Vance drew a long breath. He did not know what would happen nextвБ†вАФcould not see a fraction of an inch into the future. But in destroying the first chapters of Loot he felt as if he had torn the claws of an incubus out of his flesh. He had no idea that he had hated the book so muchвБ†вАФor was it only Tarrant he was hating when he thought of it? He flung on, flushed, defiant. He felt like a balloonist who has thrown out all his ballast: extraordinarily light and irresponsible, he bounded up toward the zenith.вБ† вБ†вА¶

As he turned the corner of his street he came upon a pedlar beating his horse. Horses were rare nowadays in New York streets, pedlars almost obsolete; but in this forgotten district both were still occasionally to be seen.вБ† вБ†вА¶ Vance stopped and looked at the load and the horse. The load was not very heavy: the horse was thin but not incapable of effort. He was not struggling against an overload, but simply balking, thrusting his shabby forelegs obstinately against the asphalt. Unknown to his driver, something was offending or torturing him somewhereвБ†вАФhe had the lifted lip and wild-rolling eye of a horse in pictures of battlefields. And the human fool stood there stupidly belabouring him.вБ† вБ†вА¶ VanceвАЩs anger leapt up. вАЬHere, you damned fool, let that horse alone, will you.вБ† вБ†вА¶вАЭ

The man, astonished and then furious, cursed back copiously in Italian and struck the horse again. вАЬAh, thatвАЩs it, is it?вАЭ Vance shouted. He caught the man by the arm, and remembering his Dante, cried out joyously: вАЬLasciate ogni speranza!вАЭ as he fell on him. The tussle was brief. He struck the whip out of the pedlarвАЩs hand, punched him in the face, and then, seeing the loafers assembling, and a policeman in the distance, suddenly remembered that it was Tarrant he had been thrashing, and shamefacedly darted away down the street to the shelter of Mrs. HubbardвАЩs door.
